Oh, what a delightful encounter it was with Suno! [laughs] He was like a breath of fresh air, bringing life to my mundane existence. Author: gkucsko
Content: the max duration is the full context, which is around ~14s for the public models. there is an EOS token which aborts sequence creation if it's produced by the model (this is to eg vocalize a short sentence) My guess is the model has usually seen only short prompts of music. It should be easy to prevent that if you use the generate functions directly and set `allow_early_stop=False`

Author: gkucsko
Content: yeah this is just a sliding window to stay somewhere in the middle of the generation and avoid edge effects. try `allow_early_stop=False` from my above answer, that should do the trick. you need the continued generation on the text-to-semantic side. the coarse model will just generate how ever long a sequence your semantic tokens are

Author: gkucsko
Content:
just added a convenient way to re-use unconditionally generated clips as input history for another generation to keep the same voice (most of the time):
```
from bark import generate_audio, save_as_prompt

text_prompt = "A first sentence said by a random speaker."
full_generation, audio_arr = generate_audio(text_prompt, output_full=True)
save_as_prompt("test.npz", full_generation)

text_prompt = "A second sentence said by the same speaker."
audio_arr_2 = generate_audio(text_prompt, history_prompt="test.npz")
```

Author: gkucsko
Content: this way people can also share good generations as `.npz` files and others can re-use them as input prompts

Author: gkucsko
Content: they work very similar to LLMs. during decoding top_k will restrict the output at every step to the most likely k results before sampling, and top_p will do the same based on cumulative probability. So you can think of them a bit similar as temperature for decoding. We haven't seen huge patterns with the parameters other than temperature, but if you find interesting stuff let us know!

Author: kmfrey
Content: Hey @RandomZombie! Nope, no cloning actually. **/sing** generates random, fully synthetic voices for each generation, which is why you’ll often get entirely different vocalists, genres, etc. from generation to generation - even when you use the same prompt. The randomly generated voices may sometimes sound similar to someone you know but not have been cloned - kind of like how many consider Dionne Bromfield and Amy Winehouse “doppelsingers” even though they're different people

Author: kmfrey
Content: It's something we've discussed internally but TBD. Yes, I know 😞 We're sympathetic to this. Some of my favorite songs from living, breathing artists wouldn't stand a chance in the server! I'm sure there's a more elegant long-term solution, but we just want to make sure we're not a vehicle (bullhorn?) for actively harmful content. We're using third-party content moderation algos at the moment, and it can be really tricky to dial those in / help them understand the diufference between creative expression and hate speech

Author: kmfrey
Content: We hear you and appreciate the feedback. The community is genearting **a lot** of songs, so manual content moderation is a tall order, but I agree some sort of appeal system would definitely be a value-add!

Author: kmfrey
Content:
Hi everyone! We’ve been experimenting with some ideas for new channels, and we’d love to get your thoughts. We’ve had some of the most fun with `/chirp` making music together —whether in-person or chatting in real-time over the phone or Zoom. 

We made the https://discord.com/channels/1069381916492562582/1164923381678866533 channel to create a space where you can chat and collaborate via voice chat while making music with `/chirp` in the sidebar.  To try it out…

1. Go to the https://discord.com/channels/1069381916492562582/1164923381678866533 channel
2. Click https://discord.com/channels/1069381916492562582/1164923381678866533 once to join the voice chat
3. Click https://discord.com/channels/1069381916492562582/1164923381678866533 a second time to open the channel
4. Click the chat icon in the top right corner
5. Use `/chirp` just like you would in public generation channels like #🐣┃chirp-beta-1 or in your DMs with the @Suno Chirp Bot

I don’t know if this is something we’ll keep but thought it could be fun to try out. Let us know what you think!

Author: kmfrey
Content:
Welcome, @SensibleWit! You can find info on getting started in the #🐣┃how-to-chirp channel, but here's the TL;DR: 

1. Go to one of the public generation channels like #🐣┃chirp-beta-1 or start a DM with the @Suno Chirp Bot 
2. Type `/chirp` and press `Enter`
3. A pop-up will appear where you can describe the style of music, enter custom lyrics, or describe a topic which GPT will help you write lyrics for 

If you want a male voice specifically, you can indicate this in the style of music field. @Jonathan Fly discovered that you can reiterate this multiple times with this model version, which seems to help. E.g., `a cappella male male male`. If you find a male voice that you like in one of your clips by happenstance, you can also use the #⏩┃continuation feature to use that voice to continue with that vibe, voice, etc.

Author: patsuno
Content: the model does tend to get confused when the lyrical genre is too discordant from the genre tags.  under the hood there knobs you can turn to influence that, but we haven't exposed these yet (it's on our roadmap).  In the long term, we'd like every generation to be a banger.  in the short term ,you can to some extent make up for it with scale (i.e. rolling several generations and picking the best one.). That's a tried and true human songwriting technique and it's useful here too.  If model output is normally distributed (who knows what it would even mean for that to be true, but *arguendo*) then best of 4 rolls gets you +1 sigma, best of 30 rolls gets you +2.

Author: patsuno
Content: to first order the biggest constraint on our strategy is that we're trying to ship a lot of features with a pretty small crew, and the timescale of model updates is shorter than the timescale of report-writing, so anything we'd publish would already be out of date

Author: patsuno
Content: also, knowledge of model arch doesn't nec help all that much with crafting good prompts-- it's very much an empirical science and I learn more from user reports here than from staring at arch diagrams tbh

Author: patsuno
Content: thanks, I have by far the least credit to take for it so far but we're all having a lot of fun building it 🙂

Content: Hey Jetta, we’ve worked closely with our lawyers on our ToS, which we wholeheartedly stand by here. Ownership is not the same as copyright, so while we're able to grant paying subscribers ownership, copyright considerations are ultimately the subscriber's responsibility to navigate — just like with other music making tools like Ableton. To that end, Suno is best suited for making new music with new lyrics.

Author: patsuno
Content: something I think a lot about is the fact that throughout music/art history, it's taken a while to figure out what the unique capabilities of a given medium are.  the first movies were just stage plays on camera, and so worse than actually existing theater.  a few decades later, you get eisenstein.  the first electric guitar music was just acoustic guitar music on an electric, and so worse than actual acoustic guitar music.  a few decades later, you get hendrix.  the stuff we're seeing now is already very cool, but I expect a similarly shaped learning curve for AI in music making.

Author: patsuno
Content:
Quick follow-up on billing issues:

Sometimes people run into billing or account-related problems.  Often, you can fix that problem yourself. If you're trying to figure out how to do that, it's fine to bring those questions to #general after reading the FAQs: https://suno-ai.notion.site/FAQs-b72601b96de44e5cacd2cd6baa985448.

But if you can't fix the problem yourself and need us to help, emailing billing@suno.ai is the fastest way to get your issue resolved: that account is hooked up to a formal tracking system that allows us to escalate it to the engineer best able to help, remedy it, and  keep tabs on it meanwhile. Posting in a channel (or DMing team members) about a problem that needs an official response can hurt more than it helps.  There's no formal tracking system in place for tracking either of those, so it's easy for the problem to just fall into a hole.

We're a small team with about 100,000 users per team member.  We're grateful to have that challenge, but it means we have to be rigorous about help requests so we can solve people's problems and have time at the end of the day to develop new models and ship new features (stay tuned!)

Author: patsuno
Content:
Hi everyone, happy Friday.

We're glad you're all here, but we're getting hugged pretty hard today.  The team's been hard at work to increase our throughput-- as you can tell, we're at the "using every part of the buffalo" stage of our GPU utilization curve right now.  We'll be back on all cylinders soon, so please sit tight.  (Or if you really can't wait, you might also consider subscribing.)

If you're concerned that you might have experienced data loss, please first be sure that you were able to play through the missing song completely at least once.  We may have dropped some generation requests today (i.e. you clicked the "Create" button but didn't get anything back), but we're not aware of any data loss for generated songs.  Please check your Library and trash (click the icon next to the "Library" heading on the Library page) and make sure you didn't accidentally dislike or trash something.  If you weren't able to generate a complete clip, you should not have been charged credits for it.

If you're still concerned about data loss after taking these steps, please email billing@suno.com with a detailed description of the problem.  Discord's nice for chat, but email is the only way we can keep track of whom we've helped and whom we haven't yet.  

Meanwhile, please be excellent to each other.

Author: patsuno
Content:
yes thanks for pointing this out, this section of the TOS is a bit out of date or at least could be clarified.

For now, the relevant distinction for sharing / privacy is the Public vs Link-Only toggle.  Public means: "I allow Suno to surface this song on the front page through algorithmically generated playlists like Trending", Link-Only means: "you have to have a link to the song in order to access it".  There is an edge case where user A gives user B a link to a Link-Only song, then user B includes it in a public playlist and now everyone can see it.  So the moral is: if you create a sensitive song, only link it to people you trust.

Author: patsuno
Content: was it ever in doubt?

Author: patsuno
Content: I mean certainly it doesn't change the ownership of the original song

Author: patsuno
Content: currently that's possible.  I think we should make it easier to see that user B extended from user A, though.  In general, you want to be able to _gain status_ from people extending your stuff, not feel _ripped off_ by it, and there are things we can do on our end to cement that.

Author: patsuno
Content: e.g. I should be able to go to your homepage and see that 100 people used your song as a jumping off point, not have 100 clones of your song swirling around and not be able to figure out who created it originally

Author: patsuno
Content: there have been some really creative, imho artistically legit uses of extending and I want to encourage a mindset of abundance rather than scarcity about it, and not just try to lock things down as much as possible

Content:
broadly speaking, the quality filter excludes:
- Profanity
- Explicit sexual content
- Juvenile or meme humor
- Political content
- References to video games, movies or TV shows
- Promotional content for commercial products or services

it's a highly imperfect solution and a lot of stuff gets caught in the dragnet.  But it's also holding back a flood of very low quality content about random cryptocoins &c

Author: patsuno
Content:
Hi all,

A quick update on the contest and manipulative behavior on the site:

- We're cognizant of the various possibilities for manipulation and are working to address potential abuse vectors both ex ante (stopping manipulation from happening in the first place) and ex post (identifying and nullifying their effects after the fact).  Some of these changes are already in effect and more are soon to come.  Some will take some time to roll out, but they'll be good for the long-term health of the site in general.

- The community's been great about responsibly disclosing vulnerabilities.  If you discover a vulnerability, please DM me with a brief summary of the issue and instructions for reproduction.  We welcome this and are glad to pay bug bounties in credits to the first user who reports any given issue.  To date, nothing's directly affected the contest leaderboard.

- Similarly, specific concrete evidence of manipulation is welcome and will be taken very seriously.  If you think you've discovered such manipulation, please DM with a brief summary so we can investigate.  It's much *less* helpful to post vague suspicions that the vibes are off 🙂

- The contest leaderboard runs according to a different, less gameable algorithm than the trending page.  We decline to say exactly how it differs, and we reserve the right to change it in response to any vulnerabilities / abuses that may arise.  Having peeked at the leaderboard so far though, the results are very listenable-- I haven't seen anything yet that we'd be embarrassed to award a prize to.

Please feel free to post questions and I'll field them throughout the day, but I'd like to encourage everyone worried about these issues that the best way to participate in the contest is just to focus on making good music.

Content: the filter isn't really 'explicitness' per se, it's more of a 'do we want to showcase this on our front page' issue.  That said, it's a temporary stopgap that we're looking forward to revising because a robot shouldn't be the one telling you what to listen to.   But on the third hand, it's also currently holding back a tide of agitprop, shootcoin shilling and worse, so it's very much a chesterton's fence.

Author: alisaadeddine_03226
Content:
Thank you to everyone who participated in The Summer of Suno, Vol. 1! We loved the songs everyone made and truly appreciate the time and creativity it takes to make great music. We understand that some users were disappointed with their placement, so I wanted to share a little more about our process. We used a private ranking algorithm and staff curatorial judgment to determine the finalists to ensure the focus was on making great music. While no ranking procedure is perfect, our criteria were straightforward, and we didn’t favor any particular users or any particular kind of music.

Some moderators made the leaderboard, and we allowed these rankings to stand as they received no special consideration. Additionally, two Suno employees also made the leaderboard  — one placed legitimately, while the other was due to an accidental programming error. We removed both songs from the list for propriety, and we’ll instead celebrate their songs internally.

To maintain fairness, we also disqualified a small number of songs that appeared to violate our ToS. However, the absence of a song from the leaderboard is not an accusation. Due to the volume of submissions, it is extremely challenging to discuss individual cases. We appreciate everyone’s understanding and sportsmanship and look forward to holding more contests in the future – can’t wait to hear more of everyone’s amazing music.

Author: kmfrey
Content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share some context on the decision to limit participation in the "Love Again" Remix Contest to the US. We've been working around the clock for weeks, trying to figure out how to expand eligibility internationally. Unfortunately, we weren't able to make it happen in time. With deadlines fast approaching, I made the difficult decision late last night to limit eligibility to the US — a choice that wasn't made lightly, especially knowing how many of our talented creators are based outside the US. 

We're always working on making our contests bigger and better and more exciting, but with that comes added complexity. This is the first contest of its kind, and we're really grateful for everyone's understanding as we work toward scaling similar contests internationally in the future.

Additionally, we've heard your feedback on previous contests, so we're trying a new approach this time with Timbaland hand-picking winners. We want to make sure we trial this new approach thoughtfully, which, combined with other factors, was another reason it's helpful to start small and expand from there. 

TL;DR: We hear you. We know this isn't ideal, and we're working on it!

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content:
so, let me first state, i'm not a lawyer, nor a legal expert, but I can offer how i'd decipher the terms in a way that I think is pretty straight ahead.

According to our terms, when you sign up, you agree that anything you submit is something that you own or have exclusive rights of. This means if you enter your own lyrics, you are telling us you own them. If you upload audio, you agree that you own it (or have exclusive rights to use it). IF you didn't have the rights to something and you broke our TOS by uploading, you would be at risk of litigation by the owner. Suno would not help you, because we very clearly state in our terms that you're not supposed to do that.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: so, the next part of your question is ownership. when you generate songs using the Basic (free plan) Suno owns the songs, and gives you personal, non-commercial use rights.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: if you made the song while subscribed, we consider you the owner, BUT, the output, is still generated by Suno, so what we do is give you commercial use rights to the song, and allow you to do what you wish (monetize it, etc)

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: in this scenario, you *could* sell a song to someone else, but you would probably be better of sublicensing the song

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: personal opinion only here: buying and selling generated music isn't something I'd get into. making the music on request and sublicensing it seems like a better business plan.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: I would say Suno doesn't get involved in this so there is no official stance. as you can see, the hypothetical is largely based on speculation, and the laws around it will inevitably move in ways that dont make it in our best interest to have an official stance.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: it's not a mystery, it seems clear that any business would stay away from conversations around reselling, it's just not our line of work. we help people make music

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: if you add your own lyrics to songs made in suno, you own those lyrics. you dont own the outputted song if you're using a free account, but you still own the lyrics

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: https://help.suno.com/en/categories/550145-rights-ownership may be helpful

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: fair. at least in the US, copyright law would indeed protect you if you wrote the lyrics and someone else attempted to use them

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: applications for moderators... I actually should have some news on how I'd like to bolster our presence for community helpers soon. Are you interested?

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: same here, but the key would be what is considered to be material that *you* make. the argument is that if you prompt a song with AI but don't write your own lyrics or play any instruments, it's not actually you making the song. songs made exclusively with AI and only basic human intervention (a prompt like "write me a song about puppies") wouldn't qualify for copyright protection in the US because neither the lyrics nor music were made by a human.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: a lawyer or subject matter expert would be helpful, but ultimately, the laws in most regions are flexible enough where most cases are determined on a case-by-case basis. as far as I can see, there may not enough closed cases around it for even experts to have a clear and concise answer.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: the difference in your case is that you'd be telling someone else to make it. a human would have to determine what that means, and create something on their own. if you told a computer to make it, there's less human element decision making, even though the content it learned from is likely human generated

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: ha i've always been mike b- there's a lot of mikes out there

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: i enjoy talking through hypotheticals- i am a daily creator who has formal education in music business, copyright law, etc. but i am not a lawyer by trade in any sense

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: loosely correct. the law in the US basically says it depends on how much you give it. which, to me, can be widely debated.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: the idea is substantial human intervention. so if you say "write me a song about puppies" the copyright office likely won't protect the output. if you used custom mode, defined the particular style, the cadence, structure of chords or lyrics or even general song form, you'd be in a much better position to be protected

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: the lawyer will only go so far, because at the end of the day, the actual generation isn't being done by a human. if you also played the song, totally different story. they'd also have to believe that your intervention is more than enough to justify you as at the very least a co-creator.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: oh sure, if you sang, it is likely the outputted generation includes some melodic and harmonic content from your input, thus putting the rights more in your favor (as long as you're not using someone elses lyrics or melody)

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: so, copyrights are complex, because the larger conversation is "do you own it" vs "will my government protect it". in a lot of regions, you "own" the copyright as soon as you make something unique. so if you wrote a lyric, you own the lyric. but, it's not protected by the government until you submit it to the copyright office. they don't have an obligation to protect your work until submitted

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: hypothetical. you and i write the same lyrics on our own pieces of paper on the same day. you choose to send yours to the copyright office, and within 6 weeks, they agree to protect your copyright. you then go and publish a song, and i say "hey those are my lyrics, i wrote them in my journal on this date". the copyright office would say "we can't determine when the other artist may have written theirs, but it was considered protected on this date". not the best way, but that's the record-keeping angle

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: interesting take! i write a lot of my own music, but have enjoyed using Suno because it will do things that I don't as a writer. there's as much to learn there as any other previously made music, so I feel like I enjoy the process of identifying what "it" did just as much as what an artist did with their live recordings

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: right, but if artist A and artist B both wholeheartedly believe that was their own creation made by themselves separately, why would they want to split ownership? and by the same recordkeeping aspect, only one of those artists gave the copyright office something to protect. its a bit flawed, but it's the best they can do with the information they have

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: I respect the callout, and appreciate that you've enjoyed using v4, but I'd have to oppose the view that giving 3.5 to all users is somehow silencing a segment of creators. As a few creators have already mentioned since your post, there have been some really great results with v3.5; it's our default model for a reason! we believe it's a fantastic gateway into creating, and I have colleagues that still prefer it over new models for certain styles. Let's change that mindset from "i'm being held back" to "here's what great music i can make with the model provided", and when there's an opportunity, you'll be able to explore new models and features that come with Pro or Premier!

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content:
I hear you, and we certainly hope our models improve each time we release! 

I hope it didn't seem like my take was from a position of looking down; as an active musician for 20 years, I'm absolutely astounded by the pace of music tech. For me personally, my gut reaction reading your take was "this stuff didn't exist two years ago, and now I'm being told one year old tech isn't good enough". It took me a minute, but I get it, because I know the line is constantly blurring (even moreso with 4.5+) and the output is getting really good.

I agree with the general sentiment here that a new point of entry for low priced subscriptions could be a great path forward. Of course, I want everyone to be wowed every time, so if I could give everybody 4.5+, I would. We'd still need to put *something* behind a paywall, though, or there wouldnt be a team left to build or maintain what you'd like to use!

but i generally write pop/rock, and influenced by music from the 60s and the 90s, primarily

Author: david_at_suno
Content:
Hey there! Thanks for reaching out!

First and foremost thank you for sharing your experience here. I hear your frustration and would like to acknoweldge that racism, abuse, antagonizing behavior and targeted insults are not acceptable in this community. Our goal is to foster a space where people can feel safe, respected, enjoy making/sharing music. 

I'd like to invite you and any others here to DM me directly with any specific cases where this is occurring. Spotlighting these occurrences will help us to take care of them swiftly.

For those that haven't seen them yet, the #📜┃rules state very clearly the expectations of this server. These points anchor us in a position where we can share this space together thoughtfully.

Again, thank you for being an advocate for this. Please never hold back from sharing your perspective on where we can improve, always looking to do so!

Author: david_at_suno
Content:
Looking forward to working through any concerns that are present. We do have something incredibly special here that can be difficult to achieve elsewhere, a place where so many different people help each other learn, grow and have fun. I've witnessed it with my own eyes for two years now. And though there definitely have been learning curves and bumps in the road, the good continues to surface. All that is because of you all, this community. 

It takes a community to community.

Okay so here's what's happening, we're starting a new initiative called **Suno Spark**! It will be an assortment of challenges that are aimed to inspire members here to create and share what they come up with!

Thanks, I'm super excited!

Rock/Metal underexposure: Aiming to change that! There are two goals with this project:

- inspire members to create music that is unique to them and who they are

- have a fair way to spotlight everyone that's participating equally

With so many people creating so many awesome songs, this has been a bit tricky to figure out. We'll be counting on you all to provide feedback on how to polish it to make it better and better to! We'll call this **phase 1** of this initiative with a strong interest to make it better and better, and where we go from there will be all about what we can figure out together!

- things that are NSFW, graphic in nature and dangerous to be on display for all ages

Hey there! Good to meet you!

Simply put, the SParks Challenge is an initiative designed to do a couple things:

- inspire members from time to time on themed guidance.
- spotlight what members here come up with through collected playlists (dedicated and tailored). Some will be surfaced through here and the suno spark channel and their will be one that will sit nicely on the Homepage to create a further reach to people passing through. 

With so many creative people here, we're trying to test ways to showcase what you all do in a fair way. To do this everyone is eligible and the rotating playlist that's showcased on the larger stage will be collected in a randomized fashion. We'll also be keeping tabs on who's been on it to ensure everyone gets a chance. Content:

With this all being so new and all of us learning so many different and cool approaches to using these tools it can be kind of tricky to lock it all in one box. Looking forward to figuring it out and learning together!

Author: david_at_suno
Content:
Hey everyone!

We’re so excited for you to check out Suno Studio! We're receiving a high volume of inquiries about pricing, etc, but let us assure you that if you've reached us via email before the sale end date of **October 5**, we will honor the pricing for you.

If you **haven't** emailed support about subscribing/upgrading to the Premier plan, please reach **billing@suno.com** and include:

- “Studio Discount” **in the subject bar** of the email
- The **email/phone number associated with your account**, and your **suno handle** (—eg @suno_handle) or a song link from the account you need help with
- A **screenshot of the discount pricing** found on suno.com/account

If you **have** sent in a support ticket:

- **Please avoid sending multiple new emails**, as this may delay our response by requiring us to reroute and merge duplicate tickets. 
- If you **did not include** the details mentioned above, feel free to gather them so you're ready when you hear back from the support team!

Thank you for your patience as we get everyone up and running; we can't wait to hear what you make with Studio!

Author: david_at_suno
Content:
Okay so here's the rules...

- You can only use one generation (10 credits)
- You create based around the theme provided and provide it here
- We'll set an allotted time frame, let's say through the next 10 minutes (give or take 😅 ) I'll drop a line when it's cut off
- The song has to be created from within this 10 minute period 
- I'll reimburse you 20 credits for sharing the song you made

The challenge theme is... **Anything Halloween-related** (take this idea and use it however you want)

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: personal opinion here: if our goal is to make music more valuable, i don't think that flooding distributors and backing them up is getting anyone more money. that said, personal value is not always monetary, which is why its nice that free users can still make great music and share it.

Content: why is it not my problem? im a musician first. toured the US, played internationally 6 days a week for several months straight. i have investment in what the landscape of music is and how we can better it for all parties

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: and you still get the outputs you paid for the way we describe them in our TOS

Author: theothermikefromsuno

Author: theothermikefromsuno

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: you're thinking of the DSP. i'm thining of the distributor. two different entities. what you may not know is that it's up to the distributor to do the base processing of information. and if they miss something it potentially becomes a hassle for everyone up the chain. and if they get 100x the volume they got two years ago because people are all trying to make money off of their free songs, theyre more likely to slip, causing another upstream of work for all parties.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: we're all one industry.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: where do you think the output came from?

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: they claimed we (suno, i imagine) was not part of the music industry. which seems silly, since we're outputting the music we're all working to defend

Content: distributors would have to change their entire business model from the ground up to control the influx theyve received. and in some instances, they have.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: this isn't new. how can Suno know what contribution is actually yours?

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content:
I think I can help here. this policy has not changed. it has always been that songs made while subscribed are granted commercial use rights, and songs made on the free plan are only available for personal, non-commercial use. it's been that way since before i started working for suno in mid 2024. 

in the TOS we note that we basically hand over any rights we had to those songs, which means you're allowed to use them however you wish and we won't seek a cut of the royalties (if you sell, distribute, or otherwise monetize them). that said, we also explicitly say we can't guarantee the output, so how could we possibly verify ownership of the song content? the output itself is generated by Suno, so we grant you commercial use rights if you made it while subscribed (aka, won't seek a cut), but ownership would involve understanding where the lyrics and other content came from, which is a whole other set of information

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: i think you're confusing owning the song, and owning the output. these are not the same. owning the song is a bit more straightforward than owning the output. if you wrote the lyrics, melody, chords, and overall song structure, there's a likelihood a court would consider you an owner of the material. that said, if you're using Suno to make a version of that material, that specific version is more just an interpolation of something you own.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: fair! to me, ownership and copyright protection go hand in hand, because a dispute for ownership really is settled in court, which relies on how material is realized (made), and how it is protected by law. by assigning rights away, it could be interpreted as considering you an owner, but realistically we'd still have no way to prove ownership over the elements of the material

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content:
this is interesting, but grey at best, and not proven in a court case that i've seen. 

here's how I'd break it down: we say you can do whatever you want with the song, so you go ahead and stem it out. you make your own mix, have real players play it, whatever. that version that you make, is yours. We said we won't take a cut, and we won't take a cut. however, if the source material is still Suno (the original output is from Suno), then you'd have a hard time justifying that Suno isn't at least a co-creator in the process if you were to take it to court. even if you wrote the lyrics yourself, you'd need to be able to clearly identify the melodic and harmonic structure as your own to truly justify the majority ownership of the original output, and even then, you'd actually be saying "oh, i already made this song, suno just interpolated it"

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content:
thanks for chiming in! the quick answer, is "suno doesn't own your piano part that you the human played into suno". the reason for that, as mentioned in the TOS, is that we expect that you are the owner or have exclusive rights to use the material you input into suno. the real long form legal version of that from the TOS is as follows:

"You represent and warrant that you own all right, title and interest in and to Submissions, including all copyrights and rights of publicity contained therein, and that you possess all necessary rights or have obtained all consents necessary to grant Suno the rights and licenses herein. By using the Service or otherwise transmitting Submissions to us, you grant to Suno and our affiliates, successors, assigns, and designees a worldwide, non-exclusive, fully paid-up, sublicensable (directly and indirectly through multiple tiers), assignable, royalty-free, perpetual, irrevocable right and license to use, reproduce, store, modify, distribute, create derivative works based on, perform, display, communicate, transmit and otherwise make available any and all Content (in whole or in part) in any media now known or hereafter developed, in connection with the provision, use, monetization, promotion, marketing, and improvement of our products and services, including the Service and the artificial intelligence and machine learning models related to the Service."

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: that sounds directionally correct. we cant guarantee that anything we output would qualify for copyright or copyright protection, but its likely a court would say "a model owned by suno made this output" and designate suno the primary contributor

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: again, i'm not attempting to claim ownership. i said earlier, there's really no way for suno to definitively assign ownership considering we cant validate input. you keep referring to the master, which i think is also a very confusing thing to try to assert, even for established music industry professionals. i'd be more inclined to say let a few court cases play out before you make an assumption of how this takes shape in formal, written, identifiable law

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: this isn't really for me to decide, but this is similar to the "i write lyrics" situation. in the US, if you write your own lyrics, when the pen hits the paper, those lyrics are yours. i'd say similarly, if you record your own unique piano part, you'd likely have claim over that part. beyond that, its maybe a little murky.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: we also say that if you make music while subscribed, you don't have to credit suno when you go to monetize it. of course, it would be nice for people to be transparent about their music making process, but that's not something we ask for currently.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: right. one of my uses for suno is literally uploading a demo so my chords, melody, and lyrics are already established as my own. suno makes the assumption that the material i upload is my own (or i have obtained exclusive rights to use it) but doesn't make a guarantee on the other side that those details are accurate, which is why this gets so murky so quickly. a court could then ask for evidence about the other material, etc, and suno really isn't involved in any of that.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: interesting! again, i like where this is going, but not sure what kind of timeline we're looking at here. to humanize it a bit, if you were in the room while sabrina carpenter was working on a song, what contribution is needed to justify credit? i have friends who are credited as assistant engineers for putting up microphones for a session on a platinum certified recording. but, in-process, say you played a shaker with a group of friends all playing percussion on a track... does your name make the liner notes but no points are awarded? that's happened plenty of times, no matter how critical those percussion parts are to the song.

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: this is an incredibly great take. his team basically always got the blessing of the artists he was parodying. it was also always in a silly way, though parody does allow for some level of pointedness. he's a master of doing things the right way

Author: theothermikefromsuno
Content: the difference between what you're describing and what suno does is the lack of output? if you mean "i gave sabrina a prompt and i gave suno a prompt, isnt that the same?" I would argue that a simple mode prompt is probably not considered "significant human contribution", the requirement for copyright protection in the US, and also likely a basis in which you could win a case for being owed a percentage based on your contribution. its tricky, for sure!
